speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] S-S. This is roll number 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: Robert, we have arrived in Palestine-- Eretz Israel. And you and the children and the other adults are now interned in Athlit for-- basically for health [INAUDIBLE] separation, whatever they call it. And then continue the story, would you please?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When we arrived in Athlit, the small Jewish Yishuv in Palestine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain Yishuv, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yishuv is-- is the Jewish people in-- in Israel, which, at that time, were very Zionistic. They were enthusiast. And they were in an-- an euphoric mood, because there were already 100 children there which knew about us. They were expecting us for four years. Even some of our children were the brothers and sisters of the children which came before them. In the meantime, those 100 which came before us, some of them had joined the British-- the Jewish British Brigade. They had fought in Italy on the other side where we were in order to liberate Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: One by the name of [? Vadal ?] died in the fighting around Ravenna and is still buried there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, they knew we are in Athlit. They came to the-- to the camp to-- to welcome us, right? And it was a tremendous feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the time where we were in Athlit, we had also to make decisions where to go. Some of the children had relatives in Palestine, and they decided to go to them, or they were taken in by them. But most of the children, including me, we decided to go to the kibbutzim. That was the way we were trained for the three, four years wandering through Europe in the hachshara system. And we thought it would be the time to give back something to the Jewish people. And by working in the kibbutzim, we could do that. And I decided to go in a kibbutzim of Hashomer Hatzair, because that was the affiliation I selected even in Vienna when I was with Aron Menczer. And some other people, like-- like-- Manny Issler, which was in [? Mizrahi, ?] which was more religious, and because of that was affiliated with [? Mizrahi. ?] He went to kibbutzim-- he went to a kibbutzim which was governed by [? Mizrahi, ?] a more religious surrounding. Some-- some of the children went to different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- first of all, I didn't know which kibbutz I should go. And one day, a selection of Hashomer [? Hatzairim ?] kibbutzim came. And it was decided by them that the kibbutz Amir in [? Galilee, ?] a rather young group of people, would be able to take 10 of the group, because they couldn't take all of them there. They were only able to take 10. Another kibbutz took another 10. And so we-- we all split up. Joschko went to his kibbutz, Gat, in the Negev. By splitting up, I would like to emphasize we really never split up, because we-- we stood-- or we are still standing, in contact with-- with each other, even if it is only through the mail now. And even if we all have glasses, and perhaps somebody is not in the very best health and we are in the-- in the 70s today, when we look out from-- with our eyes, when we see one of our people, we see only the child we were, the teenagers, whatever we did together. We never really aged. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were [? real ?] good Zionists. And when I joined the kibbutz Amir, I also joined the Palmah. The Palmah was the Jewish underground-- how should I say-- security force. There was no military force. There was no real police force, or [? something. ?] It was called-- called Palmah. And in each of the outlying kibbutzim-- and Amir was very outlying, it was in the upper [? Galilee-- ?] there was a small group, small cadre, of Palmahniks, Palmah, people of the Palmah. And we went [? through ?] a very [? rigorous ?] training. It was an [INAUDIBLE] training, because the British forces didn't allow Jews to have, legally, any weapons. Every weapon had to be obtained illegally, sometimes under very difficult and costly circumstances. And when we trained, we had to train by night and out of the sight of Arab eyes. And, you know, it was not an-- an open training. But we were very well trained. And sometimes we were called to action. And one action I have in-- in mind to-- to bring to your remembrance, one night, seven bridges were destroyed in-- in Palestine as an protest against the British taking Jewish people to the prison camp in Athlit. And when the Jordan bridge was exploded in the vicinity of Jerusalem, I was there, and I played a part [? about ?] it. At the time, we were called terrorists. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It changes. We were very active when the illegal immigration of refugees from Europe started, because the British never allowed the surviving Jews from the-- the survivors of the concentration camps from Germany and Austria coming to Italy and looking for the way to come to Palestine to come to Palestine. A matter of fact, every one ship they caught they sent either to Cyprus or to [? Mauritius-- ?] to islands. But they never let them land. But many boats were able to bring survivors in. They were taken in by the Palmah, dispersed in the kibbutzim, and they just disappeared in the country. I'm very proud of having helped that way, too. And when after, I think, a few months in Amir-- by the way, I started to learn Hebrew-- Ivrit-- after a few months, perhaps a half a year, it was decided to go on our own. We were a group of perhaps 30 teenagers. We wanted to have our own kibbutz. And we started under the name of Nahshonim in Ramat HaSharon, not far from Herzliya. We were given an small parcel of land on-- and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were given it free, but we had to pay back the money to the [? Kayemet. ?] And we started an agricultural settlement there. Later on, we built even an little factory extracting olive oil with-- with presses. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the time came and it became apparent that, sooner or later, the climate in-- in-- in Palestine will change. The British wanted to leave [INAUDIBLE]. The United Nations debated if it should come to an partition, you know. And the Arab [NON-ENGLISH], the Arab attacks on Jewish communities and on Jewish buses and the Jewish facilities, started. And, of course, the Jews reacted. And who reacted was the Palmah or the-- the Jews itself. At that time, the Jewish community decided that the Negev was needed, whatever happened. And 16 places in the Negev were built, almost overnight, as strategic points. It was not really agricultural because we couldn't do anything at the time. And in most of those points there was no water. Water had to be trucked in. And Amir-- and Ramat HaSharon, Nahshonim, our kibbutz decided to occupy one point. The point was named Chasli.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-A-S-L-I, Chasli, [? Alumim. ?] And 28 boys and girls, including me, went to the Negev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was where at this time, Robert?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ramat Gan. She was already-- she-- she didn't join the kibbutz. So when we went to the Negev, we had one-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] one concrete building, [NON-ENGLISH], it has-- that is called house and security building, three barracks out of wood, and a small water tower with perhaps two cubic meter of water. No water. But the point was 16 kilometers south of [? Beer-Sheva. ?] And by having this particular point, the Arabs never really could claim [? Beer-Sheva, ?] because [? Beer-Sheva ?] suddenly was surrounded by Jewish points. On one side was [NON-ENGLISH], Nevatim, Revivim, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. We surrounded, actually, [? Beer-Sheva. ?] We had no street going to us. The street ended in [? Beer-Sheva. ?] 15 miles was through the sand-- 15 kilometers was through the sand. And unfortunately, at the same time, the Arab fighting started. And the corridor between the northern part of Palestine and the southern part of Palestine is very, very narrow. There is only a strip of land, perhaps 4 kilometer wide, in Jewish hands between the northern and the southern part. And our-- and, of course, the road goes through. And one kibbutz, Negba, is close by actually guarding this narrow point. But we knew if this point is broken, if this strip is broken, the whole southern part of Palestine, the whole Negev, will be isolated. Strategically, if you look at the old maps, it was a nightmare. Nobody could-- nobody with-- with the right mind would believe a country can be held where a strip of land by Jerusalem is only 25 kilometer wide. 40 million Arab-- Arabs were against 800,000--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] You stay there. Stay there. [? We'll get it. ?] Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: 8,000 Jews. The key's on the side of the door. It was against the Jews-- 800,000 Jews, 800,000 Jews. But it came to that, and we had to fight, and we sustained. And I believe, looking back now, that is really what the Arabs resent most. The Arabs resent that they couldn't push the Jews into the water, because that what they say they will do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, they couldn't do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody lost from your group? Were you attacked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- we lost quite a few. In the Negev itself, we lost five people, and four or five were wounded. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But the main objective to all the War of Independence was achieved. The Negev was kept in Jewish hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank God. Thank you. How-- how long did you remain in the Negev?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Negev, completely encircled, because now the bridge between the northern part of Israel was broken. The Arabs occupied the area. We were completely surrounded for eight months. The only supply we got in was either by foot illegally through their lines or by airplanes. And the Jews were able-- the-- the Jews were able to get mostly South American Dakota--

speaker: interviewer
text: Plane.

speaker: interviewee
text: --airplanes, sometimes flown by South American pilots, or South African pilots, I should say, fly material in-- into the surrounded Negev. And that's the way the Negev actually held out. Without the help of the outside, we could never have done it. Another thing you have to consider-- none of the points, or most of the points, had no water. Water was more important to us than food. You can sustain without food. But in that heat, under [INAUDIBLE] conditions, you needed water. And, of-- of course, we rationed water. We had to truck in water. And the trucking was always a danger point, because the Arabs tried to disrupt the ways we brought in the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many months did you remain in the Negev afterwards? You said you were there for eight months. What transpired--

speaker: interviewee
text: For eight months, we were completely surrounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Israeli Defense Forces-- at that time, it was already an-- an organized army. When I was in the Palmah, we had no uniforms. We had no officers. The-- the Palmahnik selected to guide us was selected by us. He was not an officer. But Ben-Gurion decided to make a Israeli Defense Force, and he built up an army. And suddenly we had uniforms, and suddenly we got also weapons, and suddenly we got an officer corps. And suddenly, the whole thing changed. The Palmah was taken over. Our group was called the [? HaNegev ?] Battalion. And the-- the way to-- to the north was opened. The Negev actually was liberated. We went into the [? Eilat-- ?] [? Eilat ?] area, which is today [? Eilat. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was a little Arab [INAUDIBLE] point, [NON-ENGLISH] is their name. And I was one of the first ones there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We did unbelievable things. Ein Gedi, on the shores of the Dead Sea, was completely surrounded for 10 months. And we got radio messages, [INAUDIBLE] messages. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't had [? real ?] radios at that time, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: We had heliographs, where the sunlight was transmitted in-- in Morse code on visual distances. And I went in with a couple of jeeps and two trucks at one day, and we brought food. From our little supplies, we selected and we brought food to Ein Gedi. And of course, we couldn't stay there either, so we had to break through again and out. But Ein Gedi survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, did you go back to Amir?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was many times in Amir, yes. I was many times in Amir, and I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] by the way, Amir doesn't exist today anymore. They abandoned Amir.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Amir was-- the kibbutz turned into an [NON-ENGLISH]. And, you know, the kibbutzniks, which were highly idealistically when I was there and shared everything, suddenly wanted to have more individual ownership of the land. So they started to split up into-- now it's-- they [? made ?] an-- it is not an collective anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live? Where did you spend the days after the war? Where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. After the war, I went to Gedera and started to work for the-- for the [? fledglingly ?] small Israeli Air Force. And I was very close with the president of the-- of Israel today, Chaim Weizmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ezer Weizman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ezer Weizman. Chaim Weizmann was [INAUDIBLE]. Ezer Weizman, which was in the Air Force at my time, and he flew Piper-- Piper Cubs. And I went into the Air Force as a civilian. I worked in Tel Nof in the engine overhauls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But by 1951, I left Israel, and I came to the United States. And because of my experience with the Air Force, I went here to the Academy of Aeronautics. I graduated in 1957, and then I worked for 24 years for American Airlines. But the beginning of my career, actually, the airline industries in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us briefly why you left Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, out of a few reasons which compelled me to leave. I came-- and I was a real idealist. I was a real Zionist. I was an-- a youngster of perhaps 18, which went into an-- into an kibbutz which had the idea of building up the land, returning the land, which was not very productive when we got it, back to the original states of thousands of years, whatever, cedar trees and so on. And I tried-- I tried my very best. I joined the Palmah. I-- I fought in the-- in the War of Independence. I never took any money. I never had any money because everything was joined collectively. And when the war ended in 1949, I decided to leave and do-- and go on my own. And to bring you on an example, when I was with the first troops into [? Beer-Sheva ?] and we liberated-- well, not liberated, but we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we fought for [? Beer-Sheva. ?] And [? Beer-Sheva ?] was suddenly in Jewish hands, and there was no Arab left there. We found a [INAUDIBLE] fortress. We found beautiful buildings. We found also shabby Arab shacks. But everything was left like it was-- like the Arabs just fled. And everything was here for the taking. And we didn't take anything, because we-- we thought that doesn't belong to us. And we had still more fighting to do. When I came the next time back to [? Beer-Sheva, ?] that was perhaps months later, all those beautiful buildings were already occupied. And they were occupied by people from Romania, Poland-- by latecomers. I felt-- we felt not-- not people which helped us fight, but people which came afterwards, and they took profit of it. But that was not the main point. I went to work in Tel Nof. I went work for an engine overhaul. And in the-- in the meantime, I got also in contact with the-- with people which from my family survive. For example, Otto Perl, which was in the-- in Dachau, later on in-- Isle of Man, came to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I decided-- and the economic situation in '50, '51 in Israel was not a very good one. It was a very hard one, a harsh one. And thousands of new immigrants came to Palestine between '49 and '51, and that was only the first wave of immigrants. Those immigrants were not-- were not-- were not Zionists. They were not the caliber we had when I came. Those were people from Algeria, from Morocco, displaced persons from Europe. The saying was [NON-ENGLISH]-- we have no other choice. That is the reason they came. And that was the time when I say, OK, now I want to look for a better life and a different life-- let's put it this way. And I decided to come to the United States. Something else pushed a little bit. When I was in Switzerland in 1945, before knowing that I ever will get a certificate to come to the-- to Palestine legally, I put in for the Austrian quota to come to the United States. At that time, the Austrian quota took five years waiting till your number came up. In 1951, my number came up, and I could get legal entry. I could get an affidavit. So I took the opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were notified through the Austrian embassy in Tel Aviv? How did you get notice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- the notification came from the United States, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: United States [INAUDIBLE]. OK, Robert, we're coming to the end of this tape, and we'll conclude on tape number 9. Thank you.
